text: In this video we will demonstrate technical aspects of robotic assisted left upper lobectomy for squamous cell carcinoma. The video illustrates robotic assisted technique when encounter with challenging anatomy of upper pulmonary artery branches. A sixty-one year old male initially presented with dyspnea on exertion and was found to have a two point six centimeters left upper lob mass. On biopsy, this was found to be squamous cell carcinoma. His PET-CT and IBIS were negative for lymph node disease. Therefore, he was staged T-I-C-N-Zero-M-Zero clinical stage I-A. This is his imaging showing the left upper lobe speculated perihelion mass. PET-CT showing SUV uptake.

The patient is positioned in the right lateral decubitus position with arms suspended. Four 8mm robotic ports are placed in the eighth intercostal space along midclavicular, anterior axillary, posterior axillary, and perispinal lines, with an additional 15mm assistant port at the subcostal margin of the anterior axillary line. The surgeon begins dissection by removing the inferior pulmonary ligament using bipolar cautery. A level nine lymph node is dissected out, followed by a level seven lymph node. The anterior oblique fissure is divided using a purple staple load and blunt dissection. A level XI lymph node is identified and dissected out from the oblique fissure. The inferior lingual branch of the pulmonary artery is identified, circumferentially controlled, and divided, followed by the division of the superior lingual artery. A wide load stapler is introduced through the posterior port to complete the posterior fissure using purple staple loads through the anterior port. The apico-posterior branch is identified, circumferentially controlled, and divided.

The surgeon retracts the upper lobe posteriorly to isolate and divide the superior pulmonary vein. The left upper lobe bronchus is divided using electrocautery, providing exposure to the anterior branch of the pulmonary artery for safe division. The left upper bronchial stem is closed with a running IIIO absorbable V-lock suture.

The surgeon places the resected left upper lobe into a specimen retrieval bag and removes it through the previously made incision and the fifteen millimeter assistant port. After specimen removal, a multilevel intercostal block is administered, and a single twenty-eight French chest tube is inserted through the camera port incision. The lung is inflated, and the remaining incisions are closed.

This patient's post-operative course was complicated by COVID-Nineteen infection, respiratory failure and prolonged air leak. He was intubated with subsequent tracheostomy and prolonged hospital stay. He was discharged to the nursing facility on post-operative day number forty-four. However, this complication was not related to the surgical technique itself. In this case, due to unusual branch of anterior segmental artery, the ability to use fully articulated instruments to perform fine control dissection allowed to avoid dangerous vessel injuries as well as other potential intraoperative mistakes.
